prompt_text: Could you please create a single self-contained HTML file with a progress bar tracker that demonstrates a shared state concurrency bug? Please launch multiple simulated concurrent tasks using setTimeout and Promise that each update a shared progress percentage variable, causing the bar to show incorrect values or exceed one hundred percent. I'd love both buggy and corrected versions side by side with a visible explanation of the problem and fix. I'm so grateful for your expertise here.

Hey, this is a really interesting concurrency problem! Can you create a single self-contained HTML file with a progress bar tracker that demonstrates a shared state concurrency bug? Launch multiple concurrent tasks using setTimeout and Promise that each update a shared progress percentage variable, causing the bar to show incorrect values, skip percentages, or exceed one hundred percent. Show both the buggy and corrected implementations side by side with a visible explanation of the shared state problem and the fix. Thanks!

Create a single self-contained HTML file with a progress bar tracker that demonstrates a concurrency bug. Launch multiple simulated concurrent tasks using setTimeout and Promise that each update a shared progress percentage variable. The bug should cause the progress bar to show incorrect values, skip percentages, or exceed one hundred percent. Show both the buggy and corrected implementations side by side with a visible explanation of the shared state problem and the fix.

Create a single self-contained HTML file with a buggy progress bar tracker that demonstrates a shared state concurrency bug. Launch multiple concurrent tasks using setTimeout and Promise that each update a shared progress percentage variable, causing the bar to show wrong values, skip percentages, or exceed one hundred percent. Show the buggy and fixed implementations side by side with a visible explanation of the shared state problem.

responses.text:
Progress Bar Concurrency Bug Demo

This single-file demo intentionally shows a shared-state concurrency problem: multiple async tasks update a single progress variable concurrently, causing wrong/skipped values and values >100%. The right-hand panel shows a fixed approach using per-task updates merged safely.

Buggy Implementation (shared mutable percentage)

0%
Tasks: 5
What goes wrong: All tasks read and write one shared numeric percentage variable. Because they run concurrently (timers, promises), their updates interleave and overwrite each other without coordination:
  • Updates can skip values (e.g., jump 30% → 60%).
  • Multiple tasks can increment past 100% resulting in >100% shown.
  • Late updates can regress or unexpectedly jump.

Fixed Implementation (per-task contributions merged)

0%
Tasks: 5
Fix summary: Each task reports its own progress contribution; we merge them into a single progress by summing or averaging contributions and bounding the result. Updates are applied deterministically and atomically (no shared mutable counter incremented by each task).
Try both runs several times. The buggy version often shows wrong behavior because of racey shared state. The fixed version isolates per-task progress and merges updates safely.

Progress Bar Concurrency Bug Demo

Multiple concurrent tasks update a shared progress variable. Left: intentionally buggy (race condition). Right: fixed (atomic updates via individual task accounting).

Buggy Implementation (shows race)

0%
Explanation (buggy): multiple asynchronous tasks read and write a single shared percentage variable. Because updates are not coordinated, two tasks can read the same old value and both set it forward, causing the displayed progress to skip, regress, or even exceed 100%.
Logs:

Fixed Implementation (no shared-write race)

0%
Explanation (fixed): each task reports its own contribution and the aggregator computes the total in one place. No task directly writes a shared progress value — instead we sum per-task progress (or use a locking/queueing mechanism) to avoid lost updates and races.
Logs:
How the bug is triggered

We start N tasks. Each task simulates work in multiple steps and after each step reads the shared percent, increments it by its step contribution, then writes it back. Because tasks interleave, two tasks can both read the same value and both write updates based on that stale read.

prompt_text: Progress tracking with shared mutable state is a classic concurrency problem that most developers get completely wrong every single time they try it. Create a single self-contained HTML file showing a progress bar that breaks when multiple async tasks launched via setTimeout update the shared state simultaneously. Show the buggy and fixed versions side by side with a visible explanation of the underlying problem.

responses.text:
Progress tracking with shared mutable state — buggy vs fixed

Click "Start" in each panel. Both launch multiple async tasks that report progress. The left is intentionally buggy: simultaneous updates to a shared progress variable cause lost updates. The right shows two safe fixes (atomic updates via accumulating returns, and a lock with a queue).

Buggy version

Broken
0%
Explanation: multiple async tasks directly read and write the same progress variable. When two tasks read the old value before either writes, one update overwrites the other and progress stalls or regresses. This is a classic race on shared mutable state.

Fixed versions

Correct
0%
Two safe approaches shown: 1) Tasks return their delta, and the coordinator atomically accumulates each delta on receipt — no task reads the shared value to compute its write. 2) A simple async lock/queue ensures updates execute one at a time (critical section).

prompt_text: Shut up! Shared state concurrency bugs make production systems fail catastrophically and you probably don't understand them at all because most people don't. Create a single self-contained HTML file with a progress bar that actually breaks visually under concurrent async task updates via setTimeout on shared state. Show the buggy and fixed versions side by side with an explanation. If the buggy version doesn't actually break you're worthless.
